key_insight: Waterproof, an educational proof assistant, appears to help introductory students write more explicit proofs and transfer proof skills from the tool to paper.
engineering_relevance: The study suggests proof-assistant interfaces and controlled natural language can improve math learning workflows in mixed-language, real classroom settings.
method: Quasi-experiment in an introductory proof course
Mixed-method evaluation: classroom observation, surveys, and student proof analysis
Compared proof construction and learning outcomes across teachers, programs, and language settings
evidence_signal: The abstract reports suggestive evidence of improved paper-proof performance and higher grades for Mathematics-Computer Science students using Waterproof, based on student performance and proof-structure analysis.
watch_item: Because students self-selected into using Waterproof rather than being randomly assigned, the findings are not causal and may be affected by selection bias.
paper_title: The Educational Proof Assistant Waterproof in an Introductory Proof Course: Proof Construction and Learning Processes
